verses.text: Concerning the divisions of the porters: Of the Korhites was Meshelemiah the son of Kore, of the sons of Asaph. And the sons of Meshelemiah were, Zechariah the firstborn, Jediael the second, Zebadiah the third, Jathniel the fourth, Elam the fifth, Jehohanan the sixth, Elioenai the seventh. Moreover the sons of Obededom were, Shemaiah the firstborn, Jehozabad the second, Joah the third, and Sacar the fourth, and Nethaneel the fifth, Ammiel the sixth, Issachar the seventh, Peulthai the eighth: for God blessed him. Also unto Shemaiah his son were sons born, that ruled throughout the house of their father: for they were mighty men of valour. The sons of Shemaiah; Othni, and Rephael, and Obed, Elzabad, whose brethren were strong men, Elihu, and Semachiah. All these of the sons of Obededom: they and their sons and their brethren, able men for strength for the service, were threescore and two of Obededom. And Meshelemiah had sons and brethren, strong men, eighteen. Also Hosah, of the children of Merari, had sons; Simri the chief, (for though he was not the firstborn, yet his father made him the chief;) Hilkiah the second, Tebaliah the third, Zechariah the fourth: all the sons and brethren of Hosah were thirteen. Among these were the divisions of the porters, even among the chief men, having wards one against another, to minister in the house of the LORD. Guidance: David now orders the porters — the gatekeepers of the house of the LORD — and a familiar, beloved name appears among them: Obed-edom. This is the same Obed-edom in whose house the ark had rested for three months, and whom 'the LORD blessed... and all that he had' (1 Chron 13:14). Here we see the fruit of that blessing: Obed-edom has eight sons, and the Chronicler pauses to explain — 'for God blessed him' — and his sons are 'mighty men of valour,' sixty-two able men in all, given the honour of keeping the gates of God's house. The chapter is showing us the lasting reward of reverence: the man who once gladly welcomed the ark of God into his home is now blessed with a great and capable family and an honoured place in the temple service. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD remembers and rewards those who honour His presence; Obed-edom's earlier reverence, when he received the ark with godly fear, bore fruit in a household full of blessing and a generation of faithful servants. We are taught that what we do with the presence of God in our homes matters — to welcome Him reverently is to open the door to blessing for ourselves and our children. The chapter is teaching us, too, the dignity of the gatekeeper's office: these were no lowly menials but 'able men for strength,' 'mighty men of valour,' entrusted to guard the holy house. To keep the door of God's house is an honour, requiring strength, faithfulness, and watchfulness. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD has a place of service for the faithful and their descendants, and that those who honour Him in the small and private duties of reverence may find themselves and their children raised to honoured service in His house. Obed-edom, once host to the ark, now guards the temple — for God blessed him.

Concerning the divisions of the porters: Of the Korhites was Meshelemiah the son of Kore, of the sons of Asaph. And the sons of Meshelemiah were, Zechariah the firstborn, Jediael the second, Zebadiah the third, Jathniel the fourth, Elam the fifth, Jehohanan the sixth, Elioenai the seventh. Moreover the sons of Obededom were, Shemaiah the firstborn, Jehozabad the second, Joah the third, and Sacar the fourth, and Nethaneel the fifth, Ammiel the sixth, Issachar the seventh, Peulthai the eighth: for God blessed him. Also unto Shemaiah his son were sons born, that ruled throughout the house of their father: for they were mighty men of valour. The sons of Shemaiah; Othni, and Rephael, and Obed, Elzabad, whose brethren were strong men, Elihu, and Semachiah. All these of the sons of Obededom: they and their sons and their brethren, able men for strength for the service, were threescore and two of Obededom. And Meshelemiah had sons and brethren, strong men, eighteen. Also Hosah, of the children of Merari, had sons; Simri the chief, (for though he was not the firstborn, yet his father made him the chief;) Hilkiah the second, Tebaliah the third, Zechariah the fourth: all the sons and brethren of Hosah were thirteen. Among these were the divisions of the porters, even among the chief men, having wards one against another, to minister in the house of the LORD. This is the same Obed-edom in whose house the ark had rested for three months, and whom 'the LORD blessed... and all that he had' (1 Chron 13:14). The chapter is teaching us that the LORD remembers and rewards those who honour His presence; Obed-edom's earlier reverence, when he received the ark with godly fear, bore fruit in a household full of blessing and a generation of faithful servants. We are taught that what we do with the presence of God in our homes matters — to welcome Him reverently is to open the door to blessing for ourselves and our children. The chapter is teaching us, too, the dignity of the gatekeeper's office: these were no lowly menials but 'able men for strength,' 'mighty men of valour,' entrusted to guard the holy house. And they cast lots, as well the small as the great, according to the house of their fathers, for every gate. And the lot eastward fell to Shelemiah. Then for Zechariah his son, a wise counsellor, they cast lots; and his lot came out northward. To Obededom southward; and to his sons the house of Asuppim. To Shuppim and Hosah the lot came forth westward, with the gate Shallecheth, by the causeway of the going up, ward against ward. Eastward were six Levites, northward four a day, southward four a day, and toward Asuppim two and two. At Parbar westward, four at the causeway, and two at Parbar. These are the divisions of the porters among the sons of Kore, and among the sons of Merari. Guidance: The gates are assigned by lot — east, north, south, and west — 'as well the small as the great,' each family taking its post around the house of the LORD. One detail is worth noting: Zechariah, who received the northern gate, is called 'a wise counsellor.' Even among the gatekeepers, wisdom was prized and recorded. The chapter is showing us the careful, impartial assignment of every post in God's house, so that all the gates were guarded and the service fully manned, each man knowing his station. The chapter is teaching us again the fairness of the lot, 'as well the small as the great' — no family pushed to the front by power or pushed aside by weakness, but all placed by the appointment of God. The chapter is teaching us that every gate matters and every guard has his post; the worship of God is protected on every side when each faithful servant keeps his appointed ward. The chapter is teaching us, in the note that Zechariah was 'a wise counsellor,' that wisdom is a gift to be valued in every calling, even the keeping of a gate; the LORD's servants are to be not only strong and faithful but wise. The chapter is teaching us that there is honour and usefulness in guarding the entrances to God's house — keeping out what is unholy, welcoming the worshipers, watching faithfully at one's post. So in the church, those who guard the door, who watch over the flock, who keep what is holy from being profaned, do a needful and honourable work. The chapter is teaching us to take our station gladly, wherever the lot of God's providence places us — east or west, prominent or obscure — and to serve there with strength, faithfulness, and wisdom, for the LORD assigns every gate and values every faithful keeper.

And of the Levites, Ahijah was over the treasures of the house of God, and over the treasures of the dedicated things. As concerning the sons of Laadan; the sons of the Gershonite Laadan, chief fathers, even of Laadan the Gershonite, were Jehieli. The sons of Jehieli; Zetham, and Joel his brother, which were over the treasures of the house of the LORD. Of the Amramites, and the Izharites, the Hebronites, and the Uzzielites: And Shebuel the son of Gershom, the son of Moses, was ruler of the treasures. And his brethren by Eliezer; Rehabiah his son, and Jeshaiah his son, and Joram his son, and Zichri his son, and Shelomith his son. Which Shelomith and his brethren were over all the treasures of the dedicated things, which David the king, and the chief fathers, the captains over thousands and hundreds, and the captains of the host, had dedicated. Out of the spoils won in battles did they dedicate to maintain the house of the LORD. And all that Samuel the seer, and Saul the son of Kish, and Abner the son of Ner, and Joab the son of Zeruiah, had dedicated; and whosoever had dedicated any thing, it was under the hand of Shelomith, and of his brethren. Guidance: Certain Levites are appointed over 'the treasures of the house of God, and over the treasures of the dedicated things' — the offerings, the spoils of war, and all that David, his captains, and others 'had dedicated... to maintain the house of the LORD.' Shelomith and his brethren were entrusted with this sacred treasury. The chapter is showing us the careful and faithful management of the wealth devoted to God; the dedicated things were not left unguarded or handled carelessly, but placed under trustworthy stewards. The chapter is teaching us that the resources given to God's house must be managed with integrity and care — those set over the treasures bore a sacred trust, and faithfulness in handling money devoted to the LORD is itself a holy ministry. The chapter is teaching us the practice of dedicating the fruit of our labours and victories to God: 'Out of the spoils won in battles did they dedicate to maintain the house of the LORD.' What was gained, even in war, was devoted to the upkeep of God's worship — a pattern for us to consecrate our increase to His service. The chapter is teaching us, in the list of those who had dedicated treasures — Samuel, Saul, Abner, Joab, and 'whosoever had dedicated any thing' — that gifts to God's house come from many hands across many years, and all are kept and remembered; the offering of the unknown giver was stored alongside that of the famous. The chapter is teaching us that faithful stewardship of God's resources requires appointed, trustworthy keepers, accountable and diligent, for the things dedicated to the LORD are holy and must be handled as such. Whether we give or whether we are entrusted to manage what is given, the chapter is teaching us that all that pertains to God's house — even its treasury — is to be handled with reverence, faithfulness, and care, as unto the LORD.

Of the Izharites, Chenaniah and his sons were for the outward business over Israel, for officers and judges. And of the Hebronites, Hashabiah and his brethren, men of valour, a thousand and seven hundred, were officers among them of Israel on this side Jordan westward in all the business of the LORD, and in the service of the king. Among the Hebronites was Jerijah the chief, even among the Hebronites, according to the generations of his fathers. In the fortieth year of the reign of David they were sought for, and there were found among them mighty men of valour at Jazer of Gilead. And his brethren, men of valour, were two thousand and seven hundred chief fathers, whom king David made rulers over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king. Guidance: Finally, certain Levites are appointed to 'the outward business over Israel, for officers and judges' — not service within the temple, but the administration of justice and government throughout the land, 'in all the business of the LORD, and in the service of the king.' Men of valour by the thousands were set as rulers over the tribes 'for every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king.' The chapter is showing us that the Levites served God not only at the altar and in the temple courts but out in the world, as officers and judges in the civil life of the nation. The chapter is teaching us that the service of God is not confined to sacred precincts; godly men were needed in the administration of justice, in governance, in 'the outward business' of the kingdom. There is no false divide between the sacred and the secular in a rightly ordered nation: these Levites handled 'every matter pertaining to God, and affairs of the king' together, for all of life is to be lived under God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD calls some of His servants to public office, to justice and government, and that such work — when done faithfully — is as truly the service of God as ministry in the temple. The chapter is teaching us the importance of godly men in positions of civil authority: judges and officers who fear the LORD bring His righteousness into the life of the people. The chapter is teaching us, in this fitting close to the long arrangements of David, that the worship of God and the just governance of the people belong together; David provided not only for praise and sacrifice but for justice and good order throughout the land. So we are taught to serve God wherever He places us — whether in the sanctuary or in the marketplace, whether leading worship or administering justice — knowing that all faithful work done for Him, in 'the business of the LORD' and the rightful service of lawful authority, is holy in His sight.
